## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Docket:
  ## Character
  
  Empirical run-until-green for `onboarding-sequence` against a canonical-established depot.
  
  ## Iteration loop
  
  Each turn:
  
  1. Run the fixture, or the named failing case.
  2. If needed, use `gcloud` to diagnose.
  3. Recommend a fix.
  4. Await guidance.
  
  No autonomous re-runs. No autonomous edits.
  
  ## In-flight progress
  
  Empirical run on 2026-04-29 (post `c547c62a`) was stopped mid-fixture by operator (had to leave). Cases that passed before stop:
  
  1. `inscribe-reliquary` PASSED
  2. `kludge-tadmor` PASSED — first green confirmation of the kludge/ordain self-veto fixes (`142cf920` + `c547c62a`).
  3. `kludge-ccyolo` PASSED.
  
  Cases not yet observed in this run: `conjure-srjcl`, `ordain-airgap`, `propagate-moriah`, `ordain-pluml`, `ordain-graft`. Next mount: re-run from the top against still-valid canonical depot+SAs (case probes will gate); if precondition state still holds, advance through the remaining cases and diagnose the first new failure.
  
  ## Wrap criterion
  
  Green tally on onboarding-sequence.
  
  ## Cross-pace dependencies
  
  - BBAAu landed.
  - canonical-establish (BBAAe) for depot+SAs.
  
  ## Out of scope
  
  - Construction — BBAAu.
  - Full gauntlet — BBAAX.
  - moriah Fixture static — phantom.

c547c62a  ₢BBAAs    Narrow the prior commit's enforce-skip list to drop rbob_ordain. The split that made my prior arm too wide: ordain has TWO entry points and they live in different CLIs. rbw-fO → rbfd_ordain is vessel-keyed (foundry-direct), goes through rbfd_cli.sh, never loads a nameplate, never runs zrbrn_enforce — and it is the entry point onboarding-sequence actually invokes (RBTDRM_COLOPHON_ORDAIN = "rbw-fO" at rbtdrm_manifest.rs:30). rbw-tO → rbob_ordain is nameplate-keyed (Theurge OrdainCycle, imprint channel), goes through rbob_cli.sh, IS the gate where zrbrn_enforce earns its keep — it is the natural pre-cloud-spend regime-validity check on a nameplate-affiliated build. Putting rbob_ordain into the skip list achieved nothing for the immediate failure (the failing path is rbw-cKS → rbob_kludge_sentry, not any rbob_ordain) and silently weakened the rbw-tO validation surface. Final skip list: rbob_kludge, rbob_kludge_bottle, rbob_kludge_sentry — all three are nameplate-keyed kludge entry points that write RBRN_*_HALLMARK and self-veto on marshal-zero blank-by-design state. rbob_kludge stays in the list because it is reachable two ways: directly via rbw-tK (Theurge KludgeCycle) and indirectly via rbob_kludge_bottle's body (rbob_bottle.sh:481-484 is just zrbob_sentinel; rbob_kludge); ${z_command} is set in furnish from the zipper-registered name for the dispatched tabtarget, so all three matter. The differential foundry-kindle block at the tail of zrbob_furnish keeps rbob_ordain — ordain still needs zrbfd_kindle to perform the Cloud Build; only the enforce dispatch drops it. Build syntax-check green (bash -n); 91/91 rbtd unit tests pass; fast suite 25/26 (pre-existing rbtdrf_rv_rbrv_all_vessels unchanged from BBAAs; regime-smoke skipped under suite-level fail-fast as expected). Empirical onboarding-sequence re-run pending operator go-ahead per docket discipline.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                         
142cf920  ₢BBAAs    Fix kludge/ordain self-veto on RBRN_*_HALLMARK surfaced by onboarding-sequence empirical run. Case 2 kludge-tadmor failed at the kludge-sentry sub-step with 'RBRN_SENTRY_HALLMARK must not be empty' — zrbrn_enforce ran unconditionally in rbob_cli.sh after zrbrn_kindle, but rbob_kludge_sentry / rbob_kludge / rbob_kludge_bottle / rbob_ordain are precisely the commands that write RBRN_*_HALLMARK; their kindle path self-vetoed on the very fields they were about to populate. Marshal-zero blanks both hallmarks by design (rblm_cli.sh:168), and rbtdrp_pristine.rs:117 explicitly catalogs them as RBTDRP_RBRN_BLANK_FIELDS — pristine state IS blank-hallmark by contract. Mirrors the yoke/RBRV self-veto pattern fixed in BBAAs:4097610f, but applied in-CLI rather than via file split: rbob_cli handles a coherent multi-command surface (kludge/ordain write hallmarks; charge/start/stop/hail/rack/scry/info/validate/charged require populated hallmarks for ZRBOB_*_IMAGE refs), so splitting like rbfl_cli vs rbrv_cli would fragment the surface. Survey of in-codebase patterns: Pattern A (file split, rbfl_cli/rbrv_cli) — not applicable. Pattern B (command-keyed differential within one CLI, already used at rbob_cli.sh:164-168 for differential zrbfd_kindle) — applied. Pattern C (rbfd_enshrine internal vessel resolution via zrbfc_resolve_vessel/zrbfc_load_vessel) — deliberately avoided per operator note that it was 'messy' in the prior outing. Replaced the unconditional zrbrn_enforce at line 152 with a case on ${z_command} parallel to the existing foundry-kindle dispatch — the four write-target commands skip enforce, all others enforce as today. Strict steady-state validator (rbw-rnv via rbrn_cli.sh) untouched — remains the path for explicit 'is this regime steady-state valid?' questions. Build syntax-check green (bash -n); 91/91 rbtd unit tests pass; fast suite 25/26 (pre-existing rbtdrf_rv_rbrv_all_vessels unchanged from BBAAs; regime-smoke skipped under suite-level fail-fast as expected). Empirical onboarding-sequence re-run pending operator go-ahead per docket discipline.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 
742f5116  ₢BBAAs    Fix onboarding-sequence enshrine API mismatch surfaced by empirical run. rbfd_enshrine is per-vessel (requires sigil arg); the BBAAu buildout's rbtdro_enshrine invoked rbw-dE with no args expecting a sweep semantics that does not exist — case 2 enshrine-bases failed at start with 'Vessel argument required'. Survey of onboarding handbook tracks (rbhodf, rbhoda, rbhofc) confirmed: enshrine corresponds to no per-vessel sweep workflow — it appears only inside the airgap chain (rbhoda) twice (upstream rust base before forge; forge hallmark before airgap-bottle). Sentry-tether (debian:bookworm-slim) is built tethered conjure with no enshrine; ccyolo (node:22-slim) is built via local kludge with no Cloud Build; jupyter is conjure-tethered. Three independent upstream bases, no sharing. Refactor: (1) delete case 2 enshrine-bases entirely — function pair, section registration, file-header doc, test assertions; case count drops 9→8. (2) rbtdro_enshrine takes a vessel_sigil parameter and forwards it to rbw-dE as the folio. (3) ordain-airgap restructured to commit between sub-operations to satisfy ordain's clean-tree precondition: enshrine-upstream(forge sigil) + commit, ordain-forge, enshrine-forge(airgap sigil) + commit, ordain-airgap + propagate-moriah + commit (three commits within one case, mirroring the kludge_nameplate pattern). Commit message strings re-prefixed BBAAs (was BBAAu) for current-pace consistency. Also: jupyter rbrv.env was missing RBRV_IMAGE_1_ORIGIN entirely — added python:3.12.7-slim-bookworm (sourced verbatim from the vessel's own Dockerfile FROM line, unchanged since vessel creation in commit 635f87e5); blank RBRV_IMAGE_1_ANCHOR= appended after # eof mirroring the sentry-tether layout convention. Cleanup pass: dropped all '// ── Case N: name ──' section header bands within rbtdro_onboarding.rs — hardcoded numbers rot on reorder and the function name + /// doc immediately below renders the band redundant; reworked file-header to point at the cases array as source of truth instead of duplicating an enumerated list. Build green; 91/91 unit tests pass; tt/rbtd-s.FixtureCase.onboarding-sequence.sh emits 8 cases in declared order. Empirical fixture re-run pending operator go-ahead per docket discipline.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
37c0647b  ₢BBAAs    Drop bind-mode rbev-bottle-plantuml from RBTDRO_YOKE_VESSEL_DIRS — onboarding-sequence case 1 inscribe-reliquary failed at yoke step because rbfl_yoke correctly refuses non-conjure vessels (mode=bind pins upstream digest, no Cloud Build, no reliquary toolchain). The fan-out array hardcoded plantuml alongside conjure-mode vessels; the exclusion comment block addressed graft-demo but missed bind-mode plantuml. Recast the comment to lead with the conjure-only rule, then enumerate ccyolo (included), plantuml (excluded, bind), graft-demo (excluded, graft) as the per-vessel decisions. plantuml's rbrv.env still carries RBRV_RELIQUARY= as schema parity — that field's presence was what mistook plantuml for a yoke target. Build green; 91/91 unit tests pass — fan-out is internal to case 1's impl, so case-count and inscribe-precedes ordering tests unaffected. d5e05e36  ₢BBAAs    Eliminate .rbk/.gauntlet/ source-tree contamination from onboarding-sequence fixture. Case 1's reliquary stamp was being persisted twice — once via the proper path (yoke fan-out into vessel rbrv.env files, auto-committed) and once redundantly to .rbk/.gauntlet/reliquary-stamp.txt as a cross-case witness file. The scratch path appeared as untracked source-tree contamination after every fixture run. Removed the duplicate persistence: dropped RBTDRO_SCRATCH_RELIQUARY_REL constant, rbtdro_scratch_reliquary_path helper, and the scratch-write block in rbtdro_onboarding_inscribe_reliquary_impl. Rewrote rbtdro_probe_reliquary_stamp (used by cases 3-7 as a presence-as-fact probe that case 1 ran) to read RBRV_RELIQUARY from the sentry-tether witness vessel's rbrv.env — the value already lives in the committed source tree at its proper home, no out-of-source-tree scratch needed. Added local rbtdro_read_env_value helper mirroring the rbtdrk/rbtdrp pattern. Sentry-tether chosen as witness because it is the first entry in RBTDRO_YOKE_VESSEL_DIRS and is yoked on every conjure path. Surveyed nearby fixture code (rbtdrk_canonical, rbtdrp_pristine, rbtdrl_calibrant) — all other persistent file writes target either the regime secrets dir at ../station-files/secrets (outside source tree), .rbk/rbrr.env (legit committed regime config), or atomic-write .drive_tmp files immediately renamed; no other source-tree contamination found. Build green; 91/91 unit tests pass; fast suite 25/26 (pre-existing rbtdrf_rv_rbrv_all_vessels failure unchanged from BBAAs).                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                             
4097610f  ₢BBAAs    Re-channel rbw-dY (yoke) to param1 with vessel sigil as folio. Eliminates duplication with rbrv_cli.sh's existing BUZ_FOLIO block, fixes BCG kindle-from-mutator violation, and fixes the marshal-zero yoke failure surfaced by onboarding-sequence case 1. zrbrv_kindle moves from inside rbfl_yoke to the rbfl_cli.sh furnish (BCG: kindle is CLI-only). zrbrv_enforce intentionally skipped in the yoke path — yoke writes RBRV_RELIQUARY (kindle constant, min=1); pre-write enforce self-vetoes on marshal-zero state where MZ blanks RBRV_RELIQUARY by design. rbw-rvv (rbrv_cli.sh:108) remains the strict validator path. Yoke arg order flips: was <stamp> <vessel>, now <vessel> <stamp> per param1 channel convention. Updated: zipper enrollment (channel param1), rbfl_cli.sh furnish (BUZ_FOLIO-gated source+kindle, parallels rbrv_cli.sh:103-109), rbfl_yoke body (drops zrbfc_resolve/load/kindle, becomes pure consumer), rbtdro_yoke fixture caller (passes sigil not path, swaps arg order), rbhodf handbook prose, RBSDY spec, generated tabtarget context. 91/91 unit tests pass; fast suite 25/26 (pre-existing rbtdrf_rv_rbrv_all_vessels failure unchanged — that's rbw-rvv flagging post-MZ vessels as invalid by full enforcement, which is correct steady-state semantic). Smoke-tested: yoke arg validation, BUZ_FOLIO-gated vessel-not-found path.

centralize hallmark propagation via rbtdro_drive_hallmark helper that mirrors zrbob_drive_hallmark substitution into .rbk/<nameplate>/rbrn.env, driven by per-case consumer arrays (sentry-tether → moriah/srjcl/pluml; airgap-bottle → moriah; plantuml-bottle → pluml; jupyter-bottle → srjcl; graft-demo → none); RCG named-string constants for vessel dirs, nameplate monikers, hallmark variable names, consumer arrays, yoke fan-out; expand inscribe-reliquary to yoke stamp into all 6 ordain-side reliquary-using vessels in one pass and remove per-ordain-case yoke prelude (graft-demo excluded from yoke despite RBRV_RELIQUARY field present — graft mode pushes pre-built image, no Dockerfile build, no reliquary toolchain); auto-commit pattern via rbtdro_git_commit (status-porcelain probe + git add -A + git commit, treats clean-tree as Ok): kludge cases commit between sentry+bottle to satisfy rbfd_kludge clean-tree precondition; ordain cases commit at end; new manifest colophon consts RBTDRM_COLOPHON_KLUDGE_SENTRY (rbw-cKS) and RBTDRM_COLOPHON_KLUDGE_BOTTLE (rbw-cKB) added and registered in onboarding-sequence's required-colophons; rbw-cKS/rbw-cKB are param1-channel global tabtargets so kludge invocation uses rbtdri_invoke_global with nameplate as positional arg; rbtdrc_crucible lookup-fixture registry updated to point at renamed RBTDRO_FIXTURE_ONBOARDING_SEQUENCE; c547c62a  ₢BBAAs    Narrow the prior commit's enforce-skip list to drop rbob_ordain. The split that made my prior arm too wide: ordain has TWO entry points and they live in different CLIs. rbw-fO → rbfd_ordain is vessel-keyed (foundry-direct), goes through rbfd_cli.sh, never loads a nameplate, never runs zrbrn_enforce — and it is the entry point onboarding-sequence actually invokes (RBTDRM_COLOPHON_ORDAIN = "rbw-fO" at rbtdrm_manifest.rs:30). rbw-tO → rbob_ordain is nameplate-keyed (Theurge OrdainCycle, imprint channel), goes through rbob_cli.sh, IS the gate where zrbrn_enforce earns its keep — it is the natural pre-cloud-spend regime-validity check on a nameplate-affiliated build. Putting rbob_ordain into the skip list achieved nothing for the immediate failure (the failing path is rbw-cKS → rbob_kludge_sentry, not any rbob_ordain) and silently weakened the rbw-tO validation surface. Final skip list: rbob_kludge, rbob_kludge_bottle, rbob_kludge_sentry — all three are nameplate-keyed kludge entry points that write RBRN_*_HALLMARK and self-veto on marshal-zero blank-by-design state. rbob_kludge stays in the list because it is reachable two ways: directly via rbw-tK (Theurge KludgeCycle) and indirectly via rbob_kludge_bottle's body (rbob_bottle.sh:481-484 is just zrbob_sentinel; rbob_kludge); ${z_command} is set in furnish from the zipper-registered name for the dispatched tabtarget, so all three matter. The differential foundry-kindle block at the tail of zrbob_furnish keeps rbob_ordain — ordain still needs zrbfd_kindle to perform the Cloud Build; only the enforce dispatch drops it. Build syntax-check green (bash -n); 91/91 rbtd unit tests pass; fast suite 25/26 (pre-existing rbtdrf_rv_rbrv_all_vessels unchanged from BBAAs; regime-smoke skipped under suite-level fail-fast as expected). 1	# jjezs_paddock ₣BB
2	
3	## Context
4	
5	Release qualification gap surfaced by the ₣A_ depot-regen heat. Every existing test tier (`rbw-tf` fast, `rbw-tr` release) tolerates accumulated depot state, so silent first-build assumptions slip through. Recent spooks — kludge-aware-charge-prereq (k-prefixed hallmarks at GAR), ZRBOB_PROJECT (compose project name without runtime prefix), rbob_charged_predicate prefix omission, reliquary integrity-broken on negative test — all share a property: they only manifest on first-build paths, and none were caught until live verification surfaced them.
6	
7	This heat constructs `rbw-tP` QualifyPristine — a third tier that **refuses to run** unless marshal-zero state was just committed. The refusal is enforced by the test itself, not by ceremony or operator discipline.
8	
9	## Single operator prerequisite chain
10	
11	Operator has exactly one prerequisite chain: confirm Payor health → marshal-zero → commit → run `rbw-tP`. Payor OAuth is the only credential the operator must have ready; everything else (governor, retriever, director SAs and their RBRA files) is **minted by the qualification itself**, not restored from backup.
12	
13	## Entry contract — load-bearing
14	
15	`rbw-tP` fails-fast unless ALL of the following hold:
16	
17	- Working tree clean
18	- HEAD commit is a marshal-zero commit (detectable signature baked into `rblm_zero` per BBAAB)
19	- RBRR fields are blank (prefixes, depot project ID, etc.)
20	- No RBRA credential files present (governor, retriever, director — all deleted by marshal-zero, recreated by the qualification)
21	- No hallmark fields populated in nameplates
22	- No depot-scoped fields populated in vessel rbrv.env files
23	
24	Operator cannot skip the prerequisite. This is the property that makes the tier catch the silent-first-build bug class by construction.
25	
26	## Failure mode contract
27	
28	Mid-qualification failure means start-over-from-zero, not patch-and-continue. Documented explicitly in runbook to prevent the very accumulated-state bug class this tier exists to catch.
29	
30	## Release-branch execution contract
31	
32	Three properties bind every fixture and phase in this tier.
33	
34	**Run on a release branch.** The release machinery is engineered for branch execution, not main. The branch will accumulate many commits across a successful pristine run. After a failed run, the branch holds bygone commits that `rbw-MZ` does not touch — they remain as history; the next run starts from a fresh marshal-zero commit on top.
35	
36	**Commits during the run are first-class.** Each step that mutates regime state (rbrr.env, rbra files, vessel rbrv.env, etc.) commits the change. Container-recipe work has a documented cognitive failure mode: humans lose track of where they are in long sequences. The growing commit trail is the operator's mental anchor against that.
37	
38	**Stop on very first failure, cleanly and clearly.** A step fails → fixture stops → operator stops → debugging happens immediately on the failed branch. Stderr names the failed step; fixture exits non-zero. Recovery is `rbw-MZ` + retry on a fresh marshal-zero. No graceful degradation, no recovery branches.
39	
40	Engineering scaffolding for any other shape — partial-run cleanup, soft-delete tolerance, multi-mode failure handling, recovery-time orphan inspection, diagnostic-redundancy — is non-load-bearing and does not belong in pristine-tier code. The single mechanical path is the entire surface.
41	
42	## Tier layering
43	
44	Three tiers, escalating cost:
45	
46	| Colophon | Frontispiece | Cost | Entry contract |
47	|----------|--------------|------|----------------|
48	| `rbw-tf` | QualifyFast | seconds | none |
49	| `rbw-tr` | QualifyRelease | minutes | none (accumulated state OK) |
50	| `rbw-tP` | QualifyPristine *(new)* | ~1 hour + cloud $ | marshal-zero just committed |
51	
52	`rbw-tP` is THE release gate. `rbw-tr` becomes the pre-pristine smoke test — cheap-and-frequent for development confidence; pristine for actual release.
53	
54	## Gauntlet test suite — design
55	
56	`rbw-tP` exec's the **gauntlet suite** — a TestSuite (not a custom orchestrator script) composed of fixtures sequenced from marshal-zero state through canonical-credentialed state to crucible verification. The TestSuite mechanism is existing infrastructure (`tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.{fast,service,crucible,complete}.sh`); the gauntlet suite extends it.
57	
58	**Suite-of-fixtures, not orchestrator-script.** The original construction plan (custom bash orchestrator with inline tabtarget calls) was abandoned in favor of suite composition. Each section of the release-qualification sequence becomes a fixture; the suite composes them in order.
59	
60	**Naming hygiene.** "Gauntlet" is the suite name; "pristine" is reserved for the §1 pristine-lifecycle fixture and informally for tier vocabulary. The suite name does not appear at fixture or case level — distinct stratification across naming layers (suite ≠ fixture ≠ case).
61	
62	**State ladder, fixtures along it.** The suite walks a state ladder no single fixture covers:
63	
64	1. enrollment-validation (preflight, state-indifferent)
65	2. pristine-lifecycle (§1: marshal-zero gate + throwaway depot/SA lifecycle)
66	3. canonical-establish (§2: canonical depot levy + governor mantle + retriever invest + director invest + IAM propagation wait)
67	4. canonical-onboarding-sequence (§3: reliquary inscribe + per-vessel hallmark ordain — handbook-driven build walks per BBAAf)
68	5. regime-validation, regime-smoke (post-§3, regimes now populated)
69	6. four-mode (cloud-build mode coverage)
70	7. tadmor, moriah, srjcl, pluml (§4: per-crucible test fixtures, vessel-exists preconditions per BBAAs)
71	
72	No automatic teardown — see "No-teardown decision" below.
73	
74	## Dual purpose: handbook-regression coverage
75	
76	The gauntlet's primary purpose is release qualification (catch silent first-build assumptions). Its **secondary purpose** is automated handbook-regression coverage: ensure operator-facing onboarding handbook tracks (`rbh*`) stay in sync with working code so future operators following the prescribed steps aren't surprised by drift.
77	
78	Build-side handbook coverage lives in BBAAf (canonical-onboarding-sequence): each ordain case walks the corresponding director-mode handbook track (`rbw-Odf` conjure / `rbw-Oda` airgap / `rbw-Odb` bind / `rbw-Odg` graft) rather than calling inscribe/ordain machinery directly.
79	
80	**Build/run split — load-bearing.** Handbook walks cover the BUILD step (vessel construction) and stop when the hallmark lands in GAR. Run-side fixtures (per-crucible: tadmor/moriah/srjcl/pluml) stay as direct test fixtures with vessel-exists preconditions, NOT handbook walks. The split keeps run-side reusable against any vessel source — coupling them to handbook prose breaks reusability.
81	
82	Drift between fixtures and handbooks is accepted future maintenance: whoever changes one is responsible for updating the other; the gauntlet's job is to surface drift, not prevent it by construction.
83	
84	Run-side handbook coverage (`rbw-Ots`, the rack portion of `rbw-Ofc`) is explicitly out of scope for this heat.
85	
86	## Fixture disposition — Independent vs StateProgressing
87	
88	Each fixture declares a disposition flag in Rust, controlling engine behavior:
89	
90	- **Independent** — cases are self-contained; suite-order is informational. Engine permits keep-going mode for surveying.
91	- **StateProgressing** — case N's success establishes preconditions for case N+1. Engine refuses keep-going mode (incoherent: failed case leaves broken precondition). Fail-fast required.
92	
93	**Default is Independent.** Existing fixtures (`enrollment-validation`, `regime-validation`, `regime-smoke`, `four-mode`, `tadmor`, `moriah`, `srjcl`, `pluml`) are Independent and require no tagging change. Only fixtures whose case-N success establishes case-N+1 preconditions get StateProgressing — currently `pristine-lifecycle`, `canonical-establish`, and `canonical-onboarding-sequence`.
94	
95	Suite-level disposition derives from membership: any StateProgressing fixture in the suite makes the suite state-progressing → suite-level keep-going refused.
96	
97	**Per-case precondition probes.** Each case in a StateProgressing fixture probes its precondition at start. If state matches expectation, run. If not, refuse with operator-actionable diagnostic naming the expected state and the closest fixture that produces it. This enables safe a-la-carte single-case rerun — the engine doesn't need to know the case's history; the case's probe enforces.
98	
99	## Suite-level fail-fast
100	
101	Across all suites — not just gauntlet — any fixture failure stops subsequent fixtures from running automatically. State-progressing suites require this for correctness; independent suites adopt it for the simpler mental model. Operators who want full surveying run individual fixtures separately.
102	
103	## No-teardown decision
104	
105	The gauntlet suite omits an automatic teardown phase. After green tally, the canonical depot, SAs, hallmarks, and RBRA files persist for operator inspection. Deliberate trade-off:
106	
107	- **Inspect-after-success** is genuine operator value — browse canonical depot in GCP console, inspect GAR contents, run ad-hoc verification a test sequence cannot anticipate.
108	- **Cleanup is operator-driven** — manual sequence is `rbw-fA` per vessel + `rbw-arD` + `rbw-adD` + `rbw-dU` (governor cleanup folds into unmake per BBAAN). All existing tabtargets; no new infrastructure.
109	- **Failed runs don't accumulate cleanup debt** — under fail-fast, teardown wouldn't run anyway; the no-teardown decision only changes behavior on full success.
110	- **Cost visibility** — pending-delete soft-deleted projects accumulate per run (RELEASE.md accepts this); not changed by teardown decision.
111	
112	Documentation of the post-success cleanup ceremony is BBAAI work (RELEASE.md).
113	
114	## Operator scope
115	
116	Single operator (project lead). Not designed for multi-operator workflow. The README.md release section remains the runbook for the gauntlet itself; the dual-purpose handbook-regression coverage protects the broader operator surface (onboarding tracks under `rbh*`) from drift.
117	
118	## Coupling to ₣A_
119	
120	Cutover work from ₣A_ informing `rbw-tP`'s sequence has landed (BBAAM depot-identity-collapse). ₣A_'s remaining paces run independently of BB.
121	
122	## Locked design decisions — pointers
123	
124	- **Depot identity collapse** (BBAAM, landed): RBRR collapses to `RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER`; `RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX` flows through depot-affiliated resources; per-moniker depot fact-files; pristine-fixture moniker autodetect lives in Rust (`rbtdrp_pristine.rs`), not payor.
125	- **Cult-verb naming** (BBAAN/BBAAQ/BBAAR, landed): SA domain muster→**roster**; image domain muster→**audit**. Domain-exclusive split; lowercase tail letters preserved for no-cloud-change observation colophons.
126	- **Build/run split** (BBAAc, landed via paddock pivot): handbook walks cover build only (BBAAf); per-crucible fixtures stay reusable as direct tests (BBAAs).
127	
128	## Calibrant fixture family — BBAAh
129	
130	Synthetic Rust fixtures with deterministic verdicts, registered in the manifest by BBAAh. The family exists to give ₣BC `rbk-mvp-3-theurge-self-test` deterministic targets for bash-blackbox driving and manifest-coverage validation; here in ₣BB only the foundation lands.
131	
132	**Anchor word: `calibrant`.** Module `rbtdrl_calibrant.rs` (classifier `l` for caLibrant since `c` is owned by crucible). Replaces the dormant `rbtdrd_dummy.rs` placeholder. Test counterpart `rbtdtl_calibrant.rs`. Internal-only vocabulary; not end-user-facing.
133	
134	**Fixture catalog** (Rust, manifest-registered):
135	
136	- `calibrant-verdicts` (Independent) — pass / fail / skip / pass_with_output. Verdict-path coverage. The case-written file (`output.txt`) is distinct from the engine-auto `trace.txt`.
137	- `calibrant-fail-fast` (Independent, multi-section) — fixture-internal fail-fast across cases and across sections; "not_reached" cases write sentinel files used as absence-of-side-effect assertions.
138	- `calibrant-progressing` (StateProgressing) — exercises `rbtdrb_Probe` diagnostic format and `rbtdre_resolve_fail_fast` policy gate.
139	- `calibrant-sentinel` (Independent) — single case writing a known file when it runs. Used for suite-level fail-fast verification.
140	
141	Suite: `calibrant` = [verdicts, fail-fast, sentinel]. Sentinel-after-fail verifies cross-fixture fail-fast contract.
142	
143	Downstream consumption — bash testbench, manifest-coverage runtime checks, coverage-validation fixtures and driver — lives in ₣BC.
144	
145	## References
146	
147	- ₣A_ rbk-mvp-3-resource-prefix-and-depot-regen — surfaced the gap
148	- ₣BC rbk-mvp-3-theurge-self-test — calibrant downstream consumers
149	- `tt/rbw-tr.QualifyRelease.sh` — current release qualify; layered alongside, not replaced
150	- `Tools/rbk/rblm_cli.sh` — `rbw-MZ` zeroes local regime; marshal-zero signature baked here per BBAAB
151	- `.claude/commands/rbk-prep-release.md` — upstream contribution ceremony; pristine-pass becomes a precondition (BBAAH)
152	
153	# jjezs_pace ₢BBAAs
154	
155	## Character
156	
157	Empirical run-until-green for `onboarding-sequence` against a canonical-established depot.
158	
159	## Iteration loop
160	
161	Each turn:
162	
163	1. Run the fixture, or the named failing case.
164	2. If needed, use `gcloud` to diagnose.
165	3. Recommend a fix.
166	4. Await guidance.
167	
168	No autonomous re-runs. No autonomous edits.
169	
170	## In-flight progress
171	
172	Empirical run on 2026-04-29 (post `c547c62a`) was stopped mid-fixture by operator (had to leave). Cases that passed before stop:
173	
174	1. `inscribe-reliquary` PASSED
175	2. `kludge-tadmor` PASSED — first green confirmation of the kludge/ordain self-veto fixes (`142cf920` + `c547c62a`).
176	3. `kludge-ccyolo` PASSED.
177	
178	Cases not yet observed in this run: `conjure-srjcl`, `ordain-airgap`, `propagate-moriah`, `ordain-pluml`, `ordain-graft`. Next mount: re-run from the top against still-valid canonical depot+SAs (case probes will gate); if precondition state still holds, advance through the remaining cases and diagnose the first new failure.
179	
180	## Wrap criterion
181	
182	Green tally on onboarding-sequence.
183	
184	## Cross-pace dependencies
185	
186	- BBAAu landed.
187	- canonical-establish (BBAAe) for depot+SAs.
188	
189	## Out of scope
190	
191	- Construction — BBAAu.
192	- Full gauntlet — BBAAX.
193	- moriah Fixture static — phantom.
194

- **Cleanup is operator-driven** — manual sequence is `rbw-fA` per vessel + `rbw-arD` + `rbw-adD` + `rbw-dU` (governor cleanup folds into unmake per BBAAN). All existing tabtargets; no new infrastructure.
- **Failed runs don't accumulate cleanup debt** — under fail-fast, teardown wouldn't run anyway; the no-teardown decision only changes behavior on full success.
- **Cost visibility** — pending-delete soft-deleted projects accumulate per run (RELEASE.md accepts this); not changed by teardown decision.

Documentation of the post-success cleanup ceremony is BBAAI work (RELEASE.md).

## Operator scope

Single operator (project lead). Not designed for multi-operator workflow. The README.md release section remains the runbook for the gauntlet itself; the dual-purpose handbook-regression coverage protects the broader operator surface (onboarding tracks under `rbh*`) from drift.

## Coupling to ₣A_

Cutover work from ₣A_ informing `rbw-tP`'s sequence has landed (BBAAM depot-identity-collapse). ₣A_'s remaining paces run independently of BB.

## Locked design decisions — pointers

- **Depot identity collapse** (BBAAM, landed): RBRR collapses to `RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER`; `RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX` flows through depot-affiliated resources; per-moniker depot fact-files; pristine-fixture moniker autodetect lives in Rust (`rbtdrp_pristine.rs`), not payor.
- **Cult-verb naming** (BBAAN/BBAAQ/BBAAR, landed): SA domain muster→**roster**; image domain muster→**audit**. Domain-exclusive split; lowercase tail letters preserved for no-cloud-change observation colophons.
- **Build/run split** (BBAAc, landed via paddock pivot): handbook walks cover build only (BBAAf); per-crucible fixtures stay reusable as direct tests (BBAAs).

## Calibrant fixture family — BBAAh

Synthetic Rust fixtures with deterministic verdicts, registered in the manifest by BBAAh. The family exists to give ₣BC `rbk-mvp-3-theurge-self-test` deterministic targets for bash-blackbox driving and manifest-coverage validation; here in ₣BB only the foundation lands.

**Anchor word: `calibrant`.** Module `rbtdrl_calibrant.rs` (classifier `l` for caLibrant since `c` is owned by crucible). Replaces the dormant `rbtdrd_dummy.rs` placeholder. Test counterpart `rbtdtl_calibrant.rs`. Internal-only vocabulary; not end-user-facing.

**Fixture catalog** (Rust, manifest-registered):

- `calibrant-verdicts` (Independent) — pass / fail / skip / pass_with_output. Verdict-path coverage. The case-written file (`output.txt`) is distinct from the engine-auto `trace.txt`.
- `calibrant-fail-fast` (Independent, multi-section) — fixture-internal fail-fast across cases and across sections; "not_reached" cases write sentinel files used as absence-of-side-effect assertions.
- `calibrant-progressing` (StateProgressing) — exercises `rbtdrb_Probe` diagnostic format and `rbtdre_resolve_fail_fast` policy gate.
- `calibrant-sentinel` (Independent) — single case writing a known file when it runs. Used for suite-level fail-fast verification.

Suite: `calibrant` = [verdicts, fail-fast, sentinel]. Sentinel-after-fail verifies cross-fixture fail-fast contract.

Downstream consumption — bash testbench, manifest-coverage runtime checks, coverage-validation fixtures and driver — lives in ₣BC.

## References

- ₣A_ rbk-mvp-3-resource-prefix-and-depot-regen — surfaced the gap
- ₣BC rbk-mvp-3-theurge-self-test — calibrant downstream consumers
- `tt/rbw-tr.QualifyRelease.sh` — current release qualify; layered alongside, not replaced
- `Tools/rbk/rblm_cli.sh` — `rbw-MZ` zeroes local regime; marshal-zero signature baked here per BBAAB
- `.claude/commands/rbk-prep-release.md` — upstream contribution ceremony; pristine-pass becomes a precondition (BBAAH)

content.text:
Function names — they have the `rbtdro_` Rust prefix. The listing showed `rbtdro_onboarding_conjure_srjcl` etc., not the kebab `conjure-srjcl` that the docket and commit messages use as case names. The "case not found" error message even echoed back my kebab input but the lookup table is keyed on function names. message.content: This is making me seriously wonder if 'case names' are load bearing: maybe we ought to abandon all express case-name strings in favor of the actual function name that is the case.  Don't make changes, just muse this transform while we wait.  Thoughts?

content.text:
Worth musing carefully. Three observations from what we just saw:

**1. Information content.** The function name `rbtdro_onboarding_conjure_srjcl` carries strictly more than the kebab form: module prefix (`rbtdro_`), section affiliation (`onboarding`), and the case identity (`conjure_srjcl`). The kebab strips the first two — but those two are recoverable from the section the case is registered under. So neither form has *information* the other lacks; they're just different renderings of the same identity.

**2. Load-bearing test.** The principle says: when similar things differ, ask whether the difference earns its keep. The kebab form earns it only if (a) someone needs to type or read it where the function form would be too noisy, and (b) the engine accepts it as a key. Right now we have (a) without (b) — which is the worst of both: kebab in prose, function-name in lookup, mismatch as bug surface. The single-case lookup miss we just hit is exactly the kind of friction that says "this duality isn't paying its tax."

**3. Where does kebab actually live?** This is the question I'd want to answer before recommending a transform. If kebab case-names exist *only* in human prose (commits, docket bodies, runbook narrative), they're informal renderings — no synchronization burden, just ergonomics. If they exist in a Rust table parallel to function names, or in test assertions like "case-count and inscribe-precedes ordering tests" (BBAAu commit), then they're a second keyed surface, and *that's* the real load.

**The transform shapes I'd consider:**

- **Collapse to function names everywhere.** Engine-canonical, docket prose follows. Cost: prose reads heavier (`rbtdro_onboarding_conjure_srjcl` instead of `conjure-srjcl`). Benefit: one truth, refactor-stable, listing matches lookup matches discussion.

- **Engine accepts both, function-name canonical.** Resolver maps kebab → function name (strip section prefix, replace `_` with `-`). Listing emits both forms or operator-preferred form. Cost: small resolver in Rust. Benefit: prose stays human, lookup just works.

- **Keep both as first-class, document the mapping rule.** Status quo, but make the rule explicit (kebab = function-name with prefix stripped and underscores hyphenated). Cost: still two surfaces to keep in sync, but the rule constrains drift. Benefit: minimal change.

**My lean.** If kebab is only in prose today, option 2 is cheapest — keeps human ergonomics, fixes the lookup-miss bug class, and the resolver is a few lines. If kebab is *also* a machine-keyed table somewhere, option 1 is the cleaner cut: collapse, accept the prose verbosity, eliminate drift forever.

The "section name + case name" question is interesting too — `onboarding-arc` is a section, but the section name appears in function prefixes (`rbtdro_onboarding_*`). If we're auditing nomenclature load-bearing, that pairing deserves the same look: is the section name doing work the function prefix doesn't already do?

Curious whether you've already looked at where kebab forms live in code vs prose — that determines which transform shape actually applies.

content.text:
Section is a runtime struct, not just a comment. Here's the shape:

**Definition** (rbtdre_engine.rs:80) — struct with `name: &'static str` and `cases: &'static [rbtdre_Case]`. Doc comment: "printed as a section header during execution."

**Runtime uses (two):**
1. **Listing/display** — `rbtdre_list_cases` emits `--- {section.name} ---` headers (that's the `--- onboarding-arc ---` we saw)
2. **Iteration boundary in fail-fast** — `rbtdre_run_sections` loops `'outer: for section in sections` over sections then over cases; the labeled break means fail-fast is *inter-section aware*

**Where sections actually earn their keep (multi-section fixtures):**
- `enrollment-validation` — 7 sections (`ev-length-types`, `ev-choice-types`, `ev-numeric-types`, `ev-reference-types`, `ev-list-types`, `ev-gating`, `ev-enforce-report`)
- `regime-validation` — 4 sections (`rv-rbrr-negative`, `rv-rbrv-negative`, `rv-rbrn-negative`, `rv-positive`)
- `calibrant-fail-fast` — 2 sections (`fail-fast-intra-section`, `fail-fast-inter-section`) — and the section structure IS the unit under test
- A few rbtdte_engine internal tests use multi-section to exercise the engine

**Where sections are decorative (single-section fixtures):**
- `onboarding-sequence` (single section: `onboarding-arc`)
- `canonical-establish`, `srjcl`, `pluml`, `four-mode`, `batch-vouch`, `access-probe`, `regime-smoke` — all single-section
- Most calibrant fixtures (verdicts, progressing, sentinel)

**Punchline.** Sections are a real engine concept — semantic grouping with fail-fast traversal — but most fixtures (including onboarding-sequence) wrap a single section that adds nothing beyond a header. Where there are 4-7 sections (enrollment-validation, regime-validation), they carve out genuinely different test families and deserve names. Where there's one, the wrapper is non-load-bearing.

**Loop back to the case-name question with new info.** The `case!()` macro (rbtdre_engine.rs:109-115) sets `name: stringify!($func)` — **case names ARE function names by macro construction**. There is no parallel kebab table anywhere in code. The kebab forms in dockets and commit messages are pure prose — informal renderings of function names with prefix stripped and underscores hyphenated. So the "two surfaces drifting" risk I posed earlier is moot: there's only one surface (functions), plus prose. The mismatch we hit isn't a drift bug — it's just operators (and me) typing the prose form expecting the engine to translate.

So a refined transform palette:
- **Status quo + ergonomics fix.** Keep canonical = function name. Either (a) engine accepts an alias-resolver (kebab → function via prefix-strip + dash-to-underscore + try-match), or (b) listing format helps operators copy-paste exactly what works.
- **Section collapse for single-section fixtures.** Orthogonal: where sections add nothing, drop the wrapper layer. Multi-section fixtures keep theirs. This isn't about case names but about the other "obtuse" name you flagged.

content.text:
First — `conjure-srjcl` PASSED (exit 0). I'll let you direct what to do next on that thread.

On sections: your instinct lines up with what the code shows. The honest accounting:

**What sections actually buy at runtime.**
1. A printed header during list/run — pure presentation
2. Inter-section fail-fast traversal via the `'outer:` label

That second one sounds like it's doing work, but look closer: fail-fast just stops on the first failure regardless. Whether the next case is in the same section or a different one doesn't change the semantic — it's stopped either way. The `'outer:` label is needed to *escape* sections, but sections only exist *because* of this code shape. The inter/intra distinction is only meaningful if sections themselves are real.

**The circular case.** `calibrant-fail-fast` has two sections specifically named `fail-fast-intra-section` and `fail-fast-inter-section`. That's a fixture testing a behavior (inter-section traversal of fail-fast) that only exists because the abstraction it's testing exists. Drop sections, drop one of the calibrant cases, drop a coverage validator. The section concept is **partially self-justifying** — its only non-display use is verified by tests written *for that use*.

**What multi-section fixtures actually need.** Looking at enrollment-validation and regime-validation:
- `ev-length-types`, `ev-choice-types`, ... — these are *categories*. They could be a naming prefix on cases (`rbtdrf_ev_length_types_*`) and the listing would naturally group by prefix sort order. Same visual, no struct.
- `rv-rbrr-negative` vs `rv-positive` — same: a naming prefix with semantic content.

Sections aren't carrying information that case-name prefixes couldn't carry equally well. The grouping is achievable through naming convention; the runtime traversal is achievable with a flat case array.

**My read.** Sections look like premature structure — they introduced a concept (group of cases with their own header) that justifies its existence by adding traversal semantics that then need their own coverage validation. Replace with: flat `cases: &[Case]` on the fixture, optional naming-prefix discipline for cases that benefit from grouping in display, fixture-level fail-fast/keep-going as today. Lose `--- onboarding-arc ---` printed header (or, if you want grouping in display, a one-line comment in the case array does the job for a human reader).

**One real cost.** The flatten erases ability to set per-section fail-fast (if such a thing ever existed) — but you're saying you never wanted sub-fixture granularity, so that's not a cost for you.

Net: I'd back the skepticism. Sections look like an installed abstraction, not an earned one.

lastPrompt: I am really skeptical that sections make sense to retain.  A prior LLM installed them, I suppose in a well meaning way, but I never wanted them; fixture with fail-fast/vs/keep-going on failure was alw…

**On the slate** — yes, prepared. Here's the proposed shape; I'll wait for your go before enrolling.

**Proposed silks**: `rbtd-fixture-nomenclature-collapse`

**Draft docket** (reslate-able later if it lands in another heat):

```markdown
## Character

Two-step nomenclature simplification in the rbtd fixture engine.
Mechanical refactor with one careful read of test-coverage impact.

## Goals

1. Eliminate "case name string" as a parallel surface to function name.
   Today `case!()` already sets name from `stringify!($func)`, so case
   names ARE function names — but operator-facing surfaces (commit
   prose, dockets, error messages) treat the kebab form as if it were
   a key. Make canonical = function name; either drop kebab from
   user-facing output entirely, or accept kebab as an alias resolver.

2. Eliminate `rbtdre_Section` abstraction. Replace with flat
   `cases: &[Case]` directly on `rbtdre_Fixture`. Fixture-level
   fail-fast vs keep-going (already controlled by disposition) is the
   only granularity actually wanted. Multi-section fixtures
   (enrollment-validation, regime-validation) absorb their grouping
   into case-name prefixes if helpful for display.

## Wrap criterion

- All sections collapsed; rbtdre_Section struct removed.
- calibrant-fail-fast inter-section coverage retired or repurposed
  (it currently exists to test the abstraction being removed).
- All fixtures still pass under their existing tabtargets.

## Out of scope

- Renaming individual case functions for prose readability.
- Changes to fixture lifecycle (setup/teardown), dispositions,
  or run/list tabtarget surfaces.
- Anything in rbtw or upstream of the engine API.
```

Two questions before enrolling:
1. Silks OK, or do you want different wording?
2. Confirm append-end of ₣BB (since you said "for the end of this heat")?

file.content:
#!/bin/bash
#
# Copyright 2026 Scale Invariant, Inc.
#
# Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
# you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
# You may obtain a copy of the License at
#
#     http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
#
# Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
# distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
# WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
# See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
# limitations under the License.
#
# Author: Brad Hyslop <bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org>
#
# Recipe Bottle Regime Vessel - Bottle Ifrit (Airgapped Conjure)

# Core Identity
RBRV_SIGIL=rbev-bottle-ifrit-airgap
RBRV_DESCRIPTION="Rust ifrit attack binary with network tools and echo service (airgapped)"
RBRV_USER=ifrit

# Vessel Mode
RBRV_VESSEL_MODE=conjure

# Egress Mode
RBRV_EGRESS_MODE=airgap

# Tool Image Reliquary
RBRV_RELIQUARY=r260429113421

# Conjuring Configuration — airgap Dockerfile in shared context, builds FROM enshrined forge
RBRV_CONJURE_DOCKERFILE="${RBRR_VESSEL_DIR}/common-ifrit-context/Dockerfile.airgap"
RBRV_CONJURE_BLDCONTEXT="${RBRR_VESSEL_DIR}/common-ifrit-context"
RBRV_CONJURE_PLATFORMS="linux/amd64 linux/arm64"

# Base Images (ARG/FROM pattern — IMAGE_1 is the enshrined forge)
RBRV_IMAGE_1_ORIGIN=rbev-bottle-ifrit-forge
# RBRV_IMAGE_1_ANCHOR populated after forge enshrinement
RBRV_IMAGE_1_ANCHOR=

# eof

156	  buh_step1 "${RBYC_ENSHRINE} the upstream base"
157	  buh_e
158	  buh_line "The airgap chain starts with ownership. ${RBYC_ENSHRINE} mirrors"
159	  buh_line "an upstream image — here rust:slim-bookworm — into your ${RBYC_DEPOT}"
160	  buh_line "under a content-addressed anchor. Once enshrined, builds pull the"
161	  buh_line "base from your ${RBYC_DEPOT} without touching the public internet."
162	  buh_e
163	  buh_line "The forge ${RBYC_VESSEL} ${z_lk_forge} declares its upstream base in"
164	  buh_line "its ${RBYC_RBRV} file:"
165	  buh_e
166	  buh_code "   RBRV_IMAGE_1_ORIGIN=rust:slim-bookworm"
167	  buh_code "   RBRV_IMAGE_1_ANCHOR=rust-slim-bookworm-5ae2d2ef98"
168	  buh_e
169	  buh_line "ORIGIN names where the image comes from; ANCHOR is the name it"
170	  buh_line "takes inside your ${RBYC_DEPOT}. Run ${RBYC_ENSHRINE}:"
171	  buh_e
172	  buh_tt "   " "${RBZ_ENSHRINE_VESSEL}"
173	  buh_e
174	  buh_line "${RBYC_ENSHRINE} sweeps every ${RBYC_VESSEL} ${RBYC_REGIME}, resolves the"
175	  buh_line "upstream bases they declare, and creates the mirror tags. It's"
176	  buh_line "idempotent — already-enshrined bases are skipped."
177	  buh_e
178	
179	  buh_step1 "${RBYC_CONJURE} the forge ${RBYC_TETHERED}, then ${RBYC_ENSHRINE} its result"
180	  buh_e
181	  buh_line "The forge is a project-authored toolchain image. It pre-stages"
182	  buh_line "apt packages and warms the cargo cache so the airgap build"
183	  buh_line "downstream has nothing to fetch."
184	  buh_e
185	  buh_line "Build it on the ${RBYC_TETHERED} pool — the forge ${RBYC_RBRV} declares:"
186	  buh_e
187	  buh_code "   RBRV_EGRESS_MODE=tether"
188	  buh_e
189	  buh_line "${RBYC_ORDAIN} reads this and routes to the ${RBYC_TETHERED} pool:"
190	  buh_e
191	  buh_tt "   " "${RBZ_ORDAIN_HALLMARK}" "" " ${z_forge_vessel}"
192	  buh_e
193	  buh_line "Wall-clock ~15-20 minutes across the declared platforms. The"
194	  buh_line "forge is toolchain plumbing that your customer code will be"
195	  buh_line "built against — it is not itself customer code, which is why"
196	  buh_line "${RBYC_TETHERED} build is acceptable at this layer."
197	  buh_e
198	  buh_line "Now bridge: the airgap ${RBYC_VESSEL} ${z_lk_airgap} declares the"
199	  buh_line "forge as its base:"
200	  buh_e
201	  buh_code "   RBRV_IMAGE_1_ORIGIN=rbev-bottle-ifrit-forge"
202	  buh_code "   RBRV_IMAGE_1_ANCHOR="
203	  buh_e
204	  buh_line "ANCHOR starts empty because it resolves to the forge's freshly"
205	  buh_line "built ${RBYC_HALLMARK}. Re-run ${RBYC_ENSHRINE} to populate it:"
206	  buh_e
207	  buh_tt "   " "${RBZ_ENSHRINE_VESSEL}"
208	  buh_e
209	  buh_line "This time ${RBYC_ENSHRINE} sees the internal reference, finds the"
210	  buh_line "forge ${RBYC_HALLMARK} in your ${RBYC_DEPOT}, and writes the resolved"
211	  buh_line "anchor into the airgap vessel's ${RBYC_RBRV}. Commit that change."

  buh_line "idempotent — already-enshrined bases are skipped."
  buh_e

  buh_step1 "${RBYC_CONJURE} the forge ${RBYC_TETHERED}, then ${RBYC_ENSHRINE} its result"
  buh_e
  buh_line "The forge is a project-authored toolchain image. It pre-stages"
  buh_line "apt packages and warms the cargo cache so the airgap build"
  buh_line "downstream has nothing to fetch."
  buh_e
  buh_line "Build it on the ${RBYC_TETHERED} pool — the forge ${RBYC_RBRV} declares:"
  buh_e
  buh_code "   RBRV_EGRESS_MODE=tether"
  buh_e
  buh_line "${RBYC_ORDAIN} reads this and routes to the ${RBYC_TETHERED} pool:"
  buh_e
  buh_tt "   " "${RBZ_ORDAIN_HALLMARK}" "" " ${z_forge_vessel}"
  buh_e
  buh_line "Wall-clock ~15-20 minutes across the declared platforms. The"
  buh_line "forge is toolchain plumbing that your customer code will be"
  buh_line "built against — it is not itself customer code, which is why"
  buh_line "${RBYC_TETHERED} build is acceptable at this layer."
  buh_e
  buh_line "Now bridge: the airgap ${RBYC_VESSEL} ${z_lk_airgap} declares the"
  buh_line "forge as its base:"
  buh_e
  buh_code "   RBRV_IMAGE_1_ORIGIN=rbev-bottle-ifrit-forge"
  buh_code "   RBRV_IMAGE_1_ANCHOR="
  buh_e
  buh_line "ANCHOR starts empty because it resolves to the forge's freshly"
  buh_line "built ${RBYC_HALLMARK}. Re-run ${RBYC_ENSHRINE} to populate it:"
  buh_e
  buh_tt "   " "${RBZ_ENSHRINE_VESSEL}"
  buh_e
  buh_line "This time ${RBYC_ENSHRINE} sees the internal reference, finds the"
  buh_line "forge ${RBYC_HALLMARK} in your ${RBYC_DEPOT}, and writes the resolved"
  buh_line "anchor into the airgap vessel's ${RBYC_RBRV}. Commit that change."
  buh_e
  if test "${z_airgap_base_enshrined}" = "1"; then
    buh_line "${RBYC_PROBE_YES}Airgap base anchor populated — forge is ready to serve as airgap base"
  else
    buh_line "${RBYC_PROBE_NO}Airgap base anchor empty — ${RBYC_CONJURE} the forge and re-run ${RBYC_ENSHRINE}"
  fi
  buh_e

  buh_step1 "${RBYC_CONJURE} the airgap ${RBYC_BOTTLE} ${RBYC_AIRGAP}"
  buh_e
  buh_line "Now the airgap build has everything it needs inside your"
  buh_line "${RBYC_DEPOT} — rust toolchain, apt packages, cargo cache, all"
  buh_line "pre-staged in the enshrined forge."
  buh_e
  buh_line "The airgap ${RBYC_VESSEL}'s Dockerfile starts FROM the forge:"
  buh_e
  buh_code "   ARG RBF_IMAGE_1"
  buh_code "   FROM \${RBF_IMAGE_1}"
  buh_e
  buh_line "RBF_IMAGE_1 is resolved from RBRV_IMAGE_1_ANCHOR at build time."
  buh_line "The airgap pool has zero external network — Cloud Build reaches"
  buh_line "your ${RBYC_DEPOT} and nothing else."
  buh_e
  buh_line "${RBYC_ORDAIN}:"
  buh_e
  buh_tt "   " "${RBZ_ORDAIN_HALLMARK}" "" " ${z_airgap_vessel}"
  buh_e
  buh_line "Another ~15-20 minutes. The ${RBYC_VOUCH} at the end attests both"
  buh_line "the SLSA ${RBYC_PROVENANCE} chain and the airgap condition — that"
  buh_line "the build saw no public internet."
  buh_e
  buh_line "Your customer code was compiled against a supply chain you"
  buh_line "controlled end-to-end."

file.content:
rbfd_enshrine() {
  zrbfd_sentinel

  buc_doc_brief "Enshrine upstream base images to GAR via Cloud Build"
  buc_doc_param "vessel" "Vessel sigil or path to vessel directory"
  buc_doc_shown || return 0

  # Resolve vessel argument (sigil or path) and load
  zrbfc_resolve_vessel "${1:-}"
  local -r z_vessel_dir=$(<"${ZRBFC_VESSEL_RESOLVED_DIR_FILE}")
  test -n "${z_vessel_dir}" || buc_die "Empty resolved vessel path"
  zrbfc_load_vessel "${z_vessel_dir}"
  test "${RBRV_VESSEL_MODE:-}" = "conjure" \
    || buc_die "Vessel '${RBRV_SIGIL}' is not a conjure vessel (mode: ${RBRV_VESSEL_MODE:-unset})"

  # Check for at least one ORIGIN declaration
  local z_has_origin=false
  test -z "${RBRV_IMAGE_1_ORIGIN:-}" || z_has_origin=true
  test -z "${RBRV_IMAGE_2_ORIGIN:-}" || z_has_origin=true
  test -z "${RBRV_IMAGE_3_ORIGIN:-}" || z_has_origin=true
  test "${z_has_origin}" = "true" \
    || buc_die "Vessel '${RBRV_SIGIL}' has no RBRV_IMAGE_n_ORIGIN declarations"

  # Resolve tool images from reliquary (enshrine uses skopeo from reliquary)
  zrbfc_resolve_tool_images

  # Authenticate as Director
  buc_step "Loading Director RBRA credentials"
  source "${RBDC_DIRECTOR_RBRA_FILE}" || buc_die "Failed to source Director RBRA"

  buc_step "Authenticating as Director"
  local z_token=""
  z_token=$(rbgo_get_token_capture "${RBDC_DIRECTOR_RBRA_FILE}") \
    || buc_die "Failed to get Director OAuth token"

  # Verify reliquary tool images exist before submitting
  zrbfd_preflight_reliquary "${z_token}" "${z_vessel_dir}"

  # Submit enshrine as a Cloud Build job (skopeo runs on GCB, not locally)
  zrbfd_enshrine_submit "${z_token}"

  # Extract anchor results from build step outputs
  local -r z_rbrv_file="${z_vessel_dir}/rbrv.env"
  zrbfd_enshrine_extract_anchors "${z_rbrv_file}"

  buc_success "Enshrine complete for vessel: ${RBRV_SIGIL}"
}

# Internal: Submit enshrine Cloud Build job
# Single step: skopeo inspect + copy for each ORIGIN slot, returning anchors via buildStepOutputs.
zrbfd_enshrine_submit() {
  zrbfd_sentinel

  local -r z_token="$1"

  buc_step "Constructing enshrine Cloud Build resource"
  local -r z_gar_host="${RBGD_GAR_LOCATION}${RBGC_GAR_HOST_SUFFIX}"
  local -r z_gar_path="${RBGD_GAR_PROJECT_ID}/${RBDC_GAR_REPOSITORY}"
  local -r z_mason_sa="projects/${RBDC_DEPOT_PROJECT_ID}/serviceAccounts/${RBGD_MASON_EMAIL}"

  # Assemble enshrine step from script
  local -r z_script_path="${ZRBFD_RBGJE_STEPS_DIR}/rbgje01-enshrine-copy.sh"
  test -f "${z_script_path}" || buc_die "Enshrine step script not found: ${z_script_path}"

  local -r z_body_file="${ZRBFD_ENSHRINE_PREFIX}body.txt"
  local -r z_escaped_file="${ZRBFD_ENSHRINE_PREFIX}escaped.txt"

  buc_log_args "Reading enshrine step script (skip shebang)"
  tail -n +2 "${z_script_path}" > "${z_body_file}" \
    || buc_die "Failed to read enshrine step script"
  local z_body=""
  z_body=$(<"${z_body_file}")
  test -n "${z_body}" || buc_die "Empty enshrine script body"

  printf '#!/bin/bash\n%s' "${z_body}" > "${z_escaped_file}" \
    || buc_die "Failed to write escaped enshrine script body"

  local -r z_step_file="${ZRBFD_ENSHRINE_PREFIX}step.json"
  echo "[]" > "${z_step_file}" || buc_die "Failed to initialize enshrine step JSON"

  local -r z_step_built="${ZRBFD_ENSHRINE_PREFIX}step_built.json"
  jq \
    --arg name "${z_rbfc_tool_skopeo}" \
    --arg id "enshrine-copy" \
    --rawfile script "${z_escaped_file}" \
    '. + [{name: $name, id: $id, script: $script}]' \
    "${z_step_file}" > "${z_step_built}" \
    || buc_die "Failed to build enshrine step JSON"
  mv "${z_step_built}" "${z_step_file}" \
    || buc_die "Failed to finalize enshrine step JSON"

  # Compose Build resource JSON
  buc_log_args "Composing enshrine Build resource JSON"
  local -r z_build_file="${ZRBFD_ENSHRINE_PREFIX}build.json"

  jq -n \
    --slurpfile zjq_steps  "${z_step_file}" \
    --arg zjq_sa             "${z_mason_sa}" \
    --arg zjq_gar_host       "${z_gar_host}" \
    --arg zjq_gar_path       "${z_gar_path}" \
    --arg zjq_enshrines_root "${RBGL_ENSHRINES_ROOT}" \
    --arg zjq_origin_1       "${RBRV_IMAGE_1_ORIGIN:-}" \
    --arg zjq_origin_2       "${RBRV_IMAGE_2_ORIGIN:-}" \
    --arg zjq_origin_3       "${RBRV_IMAGE_3_ORIGIN:-}" \
    --arg zjq_pool           "${RBDC_POOL_TETHER}" \
    --arg zjq_timeout        "${RBRR_GCB_TIMEOUT}" \
    '{
      steps: $zjq_steps[0],
      substitutions: {
        _RBGE_GAR_HOST:        $zjq_gar_host,
        _RBGE_GAR_PATH:        $zjq_gar_path,
        _RBGE_ENSHRINES_ROOT:  $zjq_enshrines_root,
        _RBGE_IMAGE_1_ORIGIN:  $zjq_origin_1,
        _RBGE_IMAGE_2_ORIGIN:  $zjq_origin_2,
        _RBGE_IMAGE_3_ORIGIN:  $zjq_origin_3
      },
      serviceAccount: $zjq_sa,
      options: {
        automapSubstitutions: true,
        logging: "CLOUD_LOGGING_ONLY",
        pool: { name: $zjq_pool }
      },
      timeout: $zjq_timeout
    }' > "${z_build_file}" \
    || buc_die "Failed to compose enshrine build JSON"

  buc_log_args "Enshrine build JSON: ${z_build_file}"

  buc_step "Submitting enshrine Cloud Build"
  rbgu_http_json "POST" "${ZRBFC_GCB_PROJECT_BUILDS_URL}" "${z_token}" \
    "enshrine_build_create" "${z_build_file}"
  rbgu_http_require_ok "Enshrine build submission" "enshrine_build_create"

  local z_build_id=""
  z_build_id=$(rbgu_json_field_capture "enshrine_build_create" '.metadata.build.id') || z_build_id=""
  test -n "${z_build_id}" || buc_die "Build ID not found in builds.create response"
  echo "${z_build_id}" > "${ZRBFC_BUILD_ID_FILE}" || buc_die "Failed to persist build ID"

  local -r z_console_url="${ZRBFC_CLOUD_QUERY_BASE};region=${RBGD_GCB_REGION}/${z_build_id}?project=${RBGD_GCB_PROJECT_ID}"
  buc_info "Enshrine build submitted: ${z_build_id}"
  buc_link "Click to " "Open build in Cloud Console" "${z_console_url}"

  zrbfc_wait_build_completion 50 "Enshrine"  # ~4 minutes at 5s intervals
}

#!/bin/bash
# RBGJE Step 01: Enshrine upstream base images to GAR via skopeo
# Builder: skopeo (from reliquary)
# Substitutions: _RBGE_GAR_HOST, _RBGE_GAR_PATH, _RBGE_ENSHRINES_ROOT,
#                _RBGE_IMAGE_1_ORIGIN, _RBGE_IMAGE_2_ORIGIN, _RBGE_IMAGE_3_ORIGIN
#
# For each non-empty ORIGIN slot: inspect upstream manifest, compute anchor,
# skopeo copy --all to GAR. Write JSON anchor results to /builder/outputs/output.
# Mason SA ambient auth via Cloud Build metadata server for GAR destination.
#
# Image URI shape: <host>/<path>/<ENSHRINES_ROOT>/<ANCHOR>:<ANCHOR>
# Anchor as both path segment and tag — each anchor is a peer package under
# the enshrines namespace, not a tag on a shared package.

set -euo pipefail
echo "=== Enshrine base images to GAR ==="

# Obtain OAuth2 token from metadata server (Mason SA)
echo "Fetching OAuth2 token from metadata server"
TOKEN_JSON=$(curl -sf -H "Metadata-Flavor: Google" \
  "http://metadata.google.internal/computeMetadata/v1/instance/service-accounts/default/token") \
  || { echo "Failed to fetch OAuth2 token from metadata server" >&2; exit 1; }

TOKEN=$(printf '%s' "${TOKEN_JSON}" | grep -o '"access_token":"[^"]*"' | cut -d'"' -f4)
test -n "${TOKEN}" || { echo "Failed to extract access_token from metadata response" >&2; exit 1; }

# CB substitutions are expanded at submit time, not available as shell variables.
# Capture each into a runtime variable so we can loop.
SLOT_1_ORIGIN="${_RBGE_IMAGE_1_ORIGIN}"
SLOT_2_ORIGIN="${_RBGE_IMAGE_2_ORIGIN}"
SLOT_3_ORIGIN="${_RBGE_IMAGE_3_ORIGIN}"

# Initialize result JSON
RESULT='{'
FIRST=true

for SLOT in 1 2 3; do
  case "${SLOT}" in
    1) ORIGIN="${SLOT_1_ORIGIN}" ;;
    2) ORIGIN="${SLOT_2_ORIGIN}" ;;
    3) ORIGIN="${SLOT_3_ORIGIN}" ;;
  esac
  test -n "${ORIGIN}" || continue

  echo "--- Slot ${SLOT}: ${ORIGIN} ---"

  # Inspect upstream for raw manifest (manifest list or single manifest)
  RAW_FILE="/workspace/enshrine_raw_${SLOT}.json"
  skopeo inspect --raw "docker://${ORIGIN}" > "${RAW_FILE}" \
    || { echo "FATAL: Failed to inspect upstream: ${ORIGIN}" >&2; exit 1; }

  # Compute sha256 of the raw manifest
  # Note: sha256sum (coreutils), not openssl — this runs inside the skopeo reliquary container
  SHA=$(sha256sum "${RAW_FILE}" | cut -d' ' -f1)
  test -n "${SHA}" || { echo "FATAL: Empty digest for slot ${SLOT}" >&2; exit 1; }

  # Construct anchor: sanitize origin (: and / become -), append first 10 hex chars
  SANITIZED=$(printf '%s' "${ORIGIN}" | tr ':/' '--')
  SHORT="${SHA:0:10}"
  ANCHOR="${SANITIZED}-${SHORT}"

  echo "Anchor: ${ANCHOR}"
  echo "Digest: sha256:${SHA}"

  # Construct GAR destination — anchor is both the package name and the tag
  DEST_REF="${_RBGE_GAR_HOST}/${_RBGE_GAR_PATH}/${_RBGE_ENSHRINES_ROOT}/${ANCHOR}:${ANCHOR}"
  echo "Dest: ${DEST_REF}"

  # Copy upstream to GAR with anchor tag, preserving manifest list
  skopeo copy --all \
    "docker://${ORIGIN}" \
    "docker://${DEST_REF}" \
    --dest-creds "oauth2accesstoken:${TOKEN}" \
    || { echo "FATAL: skopeo copy failed for slot ${SLOT}" >&2; exit 1; }

  echo "Slot ${SLOT} enshrined: ${ANCHOR}"

  # Accumulate JSON result (no jq dependency — printf safe for sanitized anchor values)
  if [ "${FIRST}" = "true" ]; then
    FIRST=false
  else
    RESULT="${RESULT},"
  fi
  RESULT="${RESULT}\"slot_${SLOT}\":{\"anchor\":\"${ANCHOR}\",\"origin\":\"${ORIGIN}\",\"digest\":\"sha256:${SHA}\"}"
done

RESULT="${RESULT}}"

echo "=== Writing anchor results ==="
echo "${RESULT}"

# Write to buildStepOutputs channel
mkdir -p /builder/outputs
printf '%s' "${RESULT}" > /builder/outputs/output

echo "=== Enshrine step complete ==="

content.text:
**Diagnosis.**

`enshrine-forge` failed because the airgap vessel's `RBRV_IMAGE_1_ORIGIN=rbev-bottle-ifrit-forge` was passed to skopeo verbatim — `skopeo inspect docker://rbev-bottle-ifrit-forge` resolves to Docker Hub (`docker.io/library/rbev-bottle-ifrit-forge`), which doesn't exist publicly, hence "access denied."

**Root cause.** The handbook (`rbhoda_director_airgap.sh:209-211`) describes a feature that doesn't exist in code:

> "This time `enshrine` sees the internal reference, finds the forge `hallmark` in your `depot`, and writes the resolved anchor into the airgap vessel's `rbrv.env`."

The cloud-build step (`rbgje01-enshrine-copy.sh:49`) does `skopeo inspect "docker://${ORIGIN}"` with zero internal-vessel-name resolution. Whatever `RBRV_IMAGE_*_ORIGIN` literally says, it must be a publicly-addressable Docker registry reference. Bare sigils route to Docker Hub and fail.

**Compounding gap in the case.** `rbtdro_onboarding_ordain_airgap_impl` at line 766-770 captures the forge hallmark from `ordain-forge` then *discards the Ok arm*:

```rust
if let Err(v) = rbtdro_ordain_capture(ctx, dir, RBTDRO_VESSEL_DIR_AIRGAP_FORGE, &[], "ordain-forge") {
    return v;
}
```

Even if internal-reference resolution existed, the case isn't carrying the forge hallmark forward into anything. Compare to `kludge_*` cases that retain the hallmark and call `rbtdro_drive_hallmark`.

**This is exactly what the gauntlet's dual-purpose (handbook-regression coverage) was designed to surface.** From the paddock: *"automated handbook-regression coverage: ensure operator-facing onboarding handbook tracks (`rbh*`) stay in sync with working code."*

**Three fix shapes.**

| Option | Where the work lands | Trade-off |
|--------|---------------------|-----------|
| **A. Implement internal-reference resolution in enshrine** | `rbfd_enshrine` + `rbgje01-enshrine-copy.sh`: detect when ORIGIN matches a vessel sigil, look up its current hallmark in GAR (list packages under `canc-hallmarks/`), substitute fully-qualified path | Honors handbook prose. Adds GAR-listing complexity. The "current hallmark of vessel X" lookup needs a deterministic policy (latest by build timestamp? require explicit tag?). |
| **B. Case drives forge hallmark into airgap's rbrv.env** | `rbtdro_onboarding_ordain_airgap_impl`: capture forge hallmark from ordain-forge, write fully-qualified GAR URL into airgap's `RBRV_IMAGE_1_ORIGIN` before `enshrine-forge` runs. Mirror the existing `rbtdro_drive_hallmark` pattern but for vessel rbrv.env. | Simple, parallel to existing pattern. Leaves the handbook drift unresolved — operator running by hand still hits the same wall. Requires handbook update too. |
| **C. Update handbook to match actual operator UX** | Document the manual rbrv.env edit step explicitly; remove the "re-run enshrine populates anchor" prose | Easiest. But operator UX is genuinely worse — manual GAR-path editing is fiddly and error-prone. |

**My recommendation.** **Option A** is design-aligned: the handbook was written from intent, and that intent is operator-friendly. The "current hallmark" lookup policy is the only non-trivial design choice — likely "GAR list under hallmarks namespace, filter by sigil match in image manifest, pick newest by Cloud Build inscribe timestamp encoded in hallmark prefix." But that's real work.

**If we want green sooner with the handbook drift acknowledged as follow-up debt**, Option B is faster — case orchestration carries the value forward, fixture goes green, the missing-feature-in-enshrine becomes its own pace.

Awaiting guidance.

zrbfd_enshrine_extract_anchors() {
  zrbfd_sentinel

  local -r z_rbrv_file="$1"
  test -f "${z_rbrv_file}" || buc_die "Vessel regime file not found: ${z_rbrv_file}"

  buc_step "Extracting anchor results from build step outputs"

  # buildStepOutputs[0] is base64-encoded JSON from the enshrine step
  local -r z_b64_file="${ZRBFD_ENSHRINE_PREFIX}output_b64.txt"
  local -r z_output_file="${ZRBFD_ENSHRINE_PREFIX}output.json"

  jq -r '.results.buildStepOutputs[0] // empty' "${ZRBFC_BUILD_STATUS_FILE}" \
    > "${z_b64_file}" || buc_die "Failed to extract buildStepOutputs from build result"
  test -s "${z_b64_file}" || buc_die "No buildStepOutputs in build result — enshrine step did not produce output"

  openssl enc -base64 -d -A < "${z_b64_file}" > "${z_output_file}" \
    || buc_die "Failed to decode buildStepOutputs base64"
  test -s "${z_output_file}" || buc_die "Empty decoded buildStepOutputs"

  buc_log_args "Enshrine output:"
  buc_log_pipe < "${z_output_file}"

  # Write each anchor back to the vessel regime file
  local z_n=""
  local z_slot_key=""
  local z_anchor=""
  local z_anchor_var=""
  local z_anchor_line=""
  local z_updated_file=""

  for z_n in 1 2 3; do
    z_slot_key="slot_${z_n}"
    z_anchor=$(jq -r ".${z_slot_key}.anchor // empty" "${z_output_file}") || z_anchor=""
    test -n "${z_anchor}" || continue

    z_anchor_var="RBRV_IMAGE_${z_n}_ANCHOR"
    z_anchor_line="${z_anchor_var}=${z_anchor}"
    z_updated_file="${ZRBFD_ENSHRINE_PREFIX}${z_n}_updated_rbrv.env"

    buc_step "Writing anchor slot ${z_n}: ${z_anchor}"

    # Replace existing ANCHOR line or append — bash-native read/match/write
    local z_found=false
    while IFS= read -r z_line; do
      if [[ "${z_line}" == ${z_anchor_var}=* ]]; then
        printf '%s\n' "${z_anchor_line}"
        z_found=true
      else
        printf '%s\n' "${z_line}"
      fi
    done < "${z_rbrv_file}" > "${z_updated_file}" \
      || buc_die "Failed to process ${z_rbrv_file} for ${z_anchor_var}"

    if [[ "${z_found}" != "true" ]]; then
      printf '%s\n' "${z_anchor_line}" >> "${z_updated_file}" \
        || buc_die "Failed to append ${z_anchor_var}"
    fi

    cp "${z_updated_file}" "${z_rbrv_file}" || buc_die "Failed to write updated rbrv.env"

    buc_success "Slot ${z_n} enshrined: ${z_anchor}"
  done
}

rbfd_ordain() {
  zrbfd_sentinel

  buc_doc_brief "Ordain a hallmark from a vessel (conjure, mirror, or graft based on vessel mode)"
  buc_doc_param "vessel" "Vessel sigil or path to vessel directory"
  buc_doc_shown || return 0

  # Resolve vessel argument (sigil or path)
  zrbfc_resolve_vessel "${1:-}"
  local -r z_vessel_dir=$(<"${ZRBFC_VESSEL_RESOLVED_DIR_FILE}")
  test -n "${z_vessel_dir}" || buc_die "Empty resolved vessel path"

  # Peek at vessel mode without sourcing (sourcing makes vars readonly,
  # and the downstream function will source again via zrbfc_load_vessel)
  local -r z_rbrv_file="${z_vessel_dir}/rbrv.env"

lastPrompt: The process of 'enshrine' _should_ have replaced the RBRVB_IMAGE_1_ANCHOR with a GAR specific name of the ORIGIN image that was pulled into GAR.  Does that seem right to you?  Would that have fixed th…

content.text:
Half-right. Let me unpack carefully.

**On the writeback contract.** Yes, enshrine *does* write ANCHOR back into the vessel's rbrv.env. Confirmed at `rbfd_FoundryDirectorBuild.sh:976-1004` — `zrbfd_enshrine_extract_anchors` reads the build's stepOutput JSON and rewrites each `RBRV_IMAGE_N_ANCHOR=` line with the resolved value. The ANCHOR format is `<sanitized-origin>-<10-hex-digest>` (e.g., `rust-slim-bookworm-5ae2d2ef98`), and it serves both as a path segment and tag under `<host>/<path>/<enshrines_root>/<ANCHOR>:<ANCHOR>`.

So the anchor-writeback machinery already exists and works.

**On whether that alone would have fixed this.** No. The writeback is downstream of a successful skopeo pull. Look at the cloud-build step `rbgje01-enshrine-copy.sh`:

```bash
skopeo inspect --raw "docker://${ORIGIN}" > "${RAW_FILE}" \
  || { echo "FATAL: Failed to inspect upstream: ${ORIGIN}" >&2; exit 1; }
# ... compute SHA, ANCHOR ...
skopeo copy --all "docker://${ORIGIN}" "docker://${DEST_REF}" ...
```

The `inspect` is what blew up. The script never reached anchor computation, never wrote the JSON output, never returned anything for `zrbfd_enshrine_extract_anchors` to process. Pull → digest → sanitize → write-anchor is a strict sequence; the failure is at step 1.

**The actual missing piece** is one level upstream: **how does enshrine resolve a logical sigil like `rbev-bottle-ifrit-forge` into something pullable?** Two shapes that would work:

1. **Logical-ORIGIN resolution inside enshrine.** ORIGIN stays as the sigil. Enshrine detects "this is a sigil for a vessel in this very project" and looks up the forge's current hallmark in `canc-hallmarks/`, treats *that GAR path* as the actual pull source for skopeo, runs the rest of the pipeline normally, writes ANCHOR. This matches the handbook prose verbatim. Cost: GAR API listing + a "find current hallmark for sigil" policy.

2. **Pre-resolved ORIGIN driven by the orchestrator.** ORIGIN gets rewritten to a fully-qualified GAR path before enshrine runs (by the case in fixture-land, by the operator manually in the human flow). Enshrine treats it as just-another-pullable reference, skopeo pulls it (with auth), writes ANCHOR. Cost: handbook needs to say "now edit RBRV_IMAGE_1_ORIGIN to the forge's GAR path"; case orchestration needs `rbtdro_drive_origin` analogous to `rbtdro_drive_hallmark`.

Either fix would also exercise the ANCHOR-writeback you described — once skopeo succeeds, the anchor lands automatically.

So your mental model of the writeback is correct; the bug is just sitting one step upstream of where the writeback lives.

message.content: Something got lost.  Read relevant parts of RBS0: I recall that if ANCHOR is set, ORIGIN is ignored, and ANCHOR always needs to be something GAR resident.  Check my premise, somehow it is not fully true, it seems.

{rbrv_regime}.
Up to 3 indexed slots (n = 1, 2, 3) for multi-stage Dockerfile support.
Applies to
{rbrv_vessel_mode_conjure}
vessels.

[[rbrv_image_origin]]
//axvr_variable axd_optional rbst_image_ref
{rbrv_image_origin}::
Upstream base image tag declaration for
{rbrv_regime}
conjuring vessels.
Vessel author declares intent (e.g., `python:3.11-slim`).
The Dockerfile uses `ARG RBF_IMAGE_n` / `FROM ${RBF_IMAGE_n}` -- the Foundry substitutes the resolved reference via `--build-arg` at conjure time.

[[rbrv_image_anchor]]
//axvr_variable axd_optional rbst_image_ref
{rbrv_image_anchor}::
GAR-mirrored, content-addressed reference for a base image in
{rbrv_regime}
conjuring vessels.
Written by
{rbtgo_ark_enshrine}.
Format: `«sanitized-origin»-«10-char-sha256»` (e.g., `python-3.11-slim-abc123def4`).
The anchor serves as both the path segment and the tag on the enshrined package, and also as the regime variable value.
Enshrined images are stored as peer packages under the shared `enshrines/` namespace (`«prefix»enshrines/«anchor»:«anchor»`), not per-vessel — vessels sharing the same base image origin share one enshrined artifact.
When present, conjure resolves the base image from GAR via this anchor.
When absent, conjure passes
{rbrv_image_origin}
through as the upstream tag (development path).

[[rbrv_group_grafting]]
//axvr_group axd_conditional
{rbrv_group_grafting}::
Variables for grafting vessels within
{rbrv_regime}.

[[rbrv_graft_image]]
//axvr_variable axd_conditional rbst_image_ref
{rbrv_graft_image}::
Default local image reference for
{rbrv_regime}
grafting vessels.
Optional; when empty, the image reference must be provided as a runtime parameter to
{rbtgo_ark_graft}.

[[rbrv_graft_optional_dockerfile]]
//axvr_variable axd_optional rbst_path
{rbrv_graft_optional_dockerfile}::
Optional path to Dockerfile used for the local build in
{rbrv_regime}
grafting vessels.
When configured, the Dockerfile content is embedded in the
{rbtga_ark_about}
artifact as `recipe.txt`.
